story:
The door splintered and three armed aliens flooded into Jim’s cottage. One crossed the room, slapped its sucker to his forehead, and pinned him to the wall; a claw dug into his cheek while a metallic, echoing voice announced suspicion and menace. Without effort, the commander dragged him by the sucker and dropped him into his armchair, leaving a throb of pain behind his eyes. Confidential information was leaking, the alien said; sabotage had occurred.

Jim, a smallholder with a bicycle and a woodpile, protested. He only rode to the village for food and to sell firewood. The commander, unable to manage either human tone or human manners, noted that Jim had gone there that morning and signalled for a search. The soldiers overturned the single main room, glanced up at the half-loft and its ladder, then prowled through the shed, where there was nothing but the bicycle and stacks of wood. They found nothing.

Jim mentioned that he had bought bread, meat—and a cake.

“Cake?” said the commander, its voice turning oddly convivial. “You know that I have a sweet tooth.”

When Jim invited him to sit, he accepted a cup of coffee and an iced bun decorated with a pattern of letters, chewing loudly while fixing Jim with an unreadable stare. Then he rose.

“We will leave you for now,” he said. “Thank you for the cake.”

Jim watched their vehicle rattle down the hill before he began to tidy the mess.

Earlier that day, he had coasted into the village, the wind cold and clean on his face. He bought a newspaper that carried no real news anymore, not since the invasion, then took tea and did a crossword at Philip’s café. Philip chattered about shortages, petrol, and dwindling custom, polishing cups as he talked. Jim, already questioned once before, kept his voice mild and his opinions to himself.

At the butcher’s he bought sausages and a small piece of horse steak as a treat; he grew his own vegetables, but meat was harder to come by. He stepped into every shop he passed, careful to spread his errand pattern. At Henry’s bakery he picked up a rough loaf. Henry, bright-eyed, urged him to try a new wartime gâteau, sweetened without cream and studded with carrot and parsnip, then pressed on him a single iced “alphabet bun.” Each bun on the tray bore a different random-looking pattern of letters; Henry chose one carefully and slipped it into a bag.

Back home, Jim put the food away in the larder, set the bun on a plate by his chair, and studied the letters. Later he made ersatz coffee and settled down for the afternoon. He had just poured it out when the aliens arrived.

After dark, torch in hand and coat pulled tight, Jim climbed to the copse on the hilltop. By feel and habit he found the right tree, hauled himself up, and reached into a hidden fork for a very old mobile phone and a hand-crank charger. Five minutes of winding gave it enough life to send the letters by text to the single number stored in the phone. A message came back at once: OVER AND OUT.

He replaced phone and charger, trudged home, ate sausages and soup, banked the fire, and smiled at the simplicity of it all. Henry received codes by word of mouth from someone neither of them knew; Jim, a smallholder who could travel without attracting special notice, carried the letters. Neither man knew what the codes meant, so there was little either could betray. The aliens did not understand local customs well enough to question the idea of an alphabet bun. Had they studied the pattern more closely, they might have grown suspicious. Fortunately, the commander had a sweet tooth and, each time, ate the evidence before examining it.

In the morning a far-off explosion rolled across the fields. Jim hoped no innocents had been hurt. He would never learn the target. Still, the message had gone, the blast had answered, and he had done his part. He did not even like iced buns. He preferred savoury.
